architecture.summary: The system trains sparse autoencoders on frozen language model activations (residual stream, MLP, or attention head outputs) to decompose polysemantic neurons into interpretable monosemantic features. An autoencoder with a single hidden layer learns a dictionary of feature directions via L1-regularized reconstruction loss; the hidden activations (coefficients) represent sparse linear combinations of dictionary features that reconstruct the original activation vectors. Information flows: frozen LM activations → encoder projects to feature coefficients → decoder reconstructs via sparse dictionary.
trained_components: sparse autoencoder (encoder and decoder weight matrices with tied weights)

biblio.title: Sparse Autoencoders Find Highly Interpretable Features in Language Models

claims.statement: Sparse dictionary features are far more interpretable by autointerpretability score than dictionary features found by comparable techniques (default basis, random directions, PCA, ICA)
The strength of interpretability improvement over baselines declines as we move through the model, being comparable to ICA in layer 4 and showing minimal improvement in the final layer

Patching sparse dictionary features requires fewer patches to reach a given level of KL divergence from target compared to PCA decomposition and non-sparse dictionary on the Indirect Object Identification task

claims.dataset: Pythia-70M residual stream; case studies on layer 1 and layer 5 features
claims.statement: Dictionary features are highly monosemantic: individual features activate on semantically coherent contexts such as apostrophes only, periods only, the token 'the', or newline characters

claims.dataset: Pythia-70M; apostrophe feature (layer 1) reduces logit for following 's' token
claims.statement: Ablating dictionary features produces intuitive effects on model output logits consistent with the feature's detected semantic content

claims.dataset: Pile; Pythia-70M layer 2 residual stream substitution
claims.statement: Sparse autoencoders do not achieve zero reconstruction loss; replacing residual stream activations in layer 2 of Pythia-70M with autoencoder reconstruction increases perplexity on the Pile from 25 to 40
Current methods for training sparse autoencoders are best suited to the residual stream; the training pipeline is not able to robustly learn overcomplete bases in the intermediate layers of the MLP

claims.dataset: Pythia-70M and Pythia-410M on Pile; sparse autoencoder training
claims.statement: A single training run using 5-50M activation vectors completes in under an hour on a single A40 GPU
